titleStmt.title: Letter from Herbert Pim to John Sweetman, 12 October 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Herbert Pim

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Herbert Pim (1883-1950) to John Sweetman (1884-1936). Pim published a pamphlet series entitled 'Tracts of our times', including writings by Patrick Pearse, and in early 1916 founded the literary and political monthly, 'The Irishman'. He joined the Volunteer muster in Coalisland at Easter 1916, and was arrested after the Rising and deported to Reading gaol. He was released in September 1916. Sweetman was a member of Sinn Féin and patron to Arthur Griffith. In 1915 he spoke out against conscription and was arrested after the 1916 Rising and interned in England until the end of May 1916. Pim is sending Sweetman a copy of correspondence with Alderman Tom Kelly (1868-1942) and is confirming to Sweetman that he has decided to take his advice in a matter. Pim also discusses how he has killed the Nation League.

msIdentifier.collection: Sweetman Family Papers/ Collection List No 156; Ms. 47589/12/9

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
65, University Road, Belfast, 12th October 1916. My dear Mr. Sweetman, I enclose copy of my correspondence with
Alderman Kelly, who apparenly represents the existing council
members. You will observe that I have taken the line which
your foresight predicted that I should be compelled to take. Please return the correspondence. I hope the current
issue of The Irishman pleases you. We have killed the Nation
League, at any rate, if we may judge by the meeting which broke
up in Dublin last night. This disaster was not reported in the
Independent, which must feel pretty sick at having backed the
wrong horse. With kindest regards,
Yours very sincerely, Herbert M Pim
